does: This paper investigates self-learning mechanisms for LLM-based agents in search environments without relying on human-annotated data or fixed reward rules. The authors conduct controlled experiments to identify key scaling factors: reward signal source and task data volume. They introduce Agentic Self-Learning (ASL), a closed-loop reinforcement learning framework where three LLM-based components—a Prompt Generator, a Policy Model, and a Generative Reward Model—collaboratively create a virtuous cycle. The Prompt Generator synthesizes diverse tasks, the Policy Model learns to solve them, and the Generative Reward Model provides adaptive feedback. Empirically, ASL demonstrates that rewards from a co-evolved Generative Reward Model outperform rigid rule-based signals, and that increasing synthetic task data substantially enhances agent capabilities and sustains performance improvements across training rounds.
connects: This work builds on prior research in reinforcement learning for language models, particularly efforts to move beyond supervised fine-tuning toward agents that learn from task execution. It extends recent work on reward modeling and self-improvement in LLMs, responding to limitations of fixed reward functions for open-domain learning. The paper connects to multi-agent reinforcement learning frameworks and curriculum learning approaches, where agents progressively tackle harder problems. It also builds on generative reward modeling literature, which has emerged as an alternative to hand-crafted reward functions. The search environment setting grounds the work in practical agent evaluation benchmarks. The emphasis on scaling without human curation responds to data scarcity challenges in LLM training and aligns with trends toward autonomous data generation and synthetic supervision.
enables: This work opens directions for fully autonomous agent training systems that eliminate human annotation bottlenecks, scaling language model capabilities toward genuine self-improvement. The closed-loop framework suggests potential for agents that continuously refine their own task distributions and evaluation criteria, potentially enabling deployment in dynamic, unseen domains. The insights on Generative Reward Models could extend beyond search to other agentic domains, supporting development of adaptive reward mechanisms that co-evolve with policy learning. Future work might investigate stability and convergence properties of multi-role self-learning systems, or extend ASL to multi-agent settings where agents generate tasks for one another. The framework also points toward research on task diversity and curriculum quality in synthetic data generation, and could motivate investigation into how agents discover and represent their own failure modes for continuous improvement.
